text:
St Mary's Hospital

Institute of Neurology

Clinic Date: 14 October 2025

Dr Hannah McKenzie
Greenford Road Medical Practice
214 Greenford Road, London, UB6 8QN

Dear Dr Hannah McKenzie,

Sophie Bennett, DOB: 12-03-1991, Hospital No: H482930 NHS No. 9073615248
Flat 3, 29 Larkspur Court, London, W5 4TA

Reason for review:
Follow-up after recent long-haul travel with reported sleep disruption, jet lag, and perceived worsening of events.

History since last clinic:
Following cross-time-zone travel to visit family, she reports 10–14 days of circadian disruption with fragmented sleep and heightened anxiety about further events. She describes increased morning myoclonic twitches and more frequent brief lapses in attention during the first fortnight after return. She has also noted headaches and irritability when sleep debt accumulates. No injuries, tongue biting, or incontinence reported. She maintains good medication adherence (no missed doses reported) and denies intercurrent illness or alcohol excess. A family member has begun keeping a simple diary of events and sleep duration.

Seizure description and frequency:
There has been little alteration in seizure frequency over the past year; she experiences one to two generalised tonic-clonic seizures yearly with the longest seizure-free period being three weeks. She has three - four absences per day. In addition, she suffers clusters of myoclonic jerks and occasional tonic seizures, these occur roughly once a month. No seizures have been recorded since her last appointment.

Despite the above baseline, she perceives deterioration in the days following the flight, particularly with early morning jerks and daytime lapses, coinciding with reduced total sleep and shifting sleep–wake times. She feels overall confidence has worsened and is avoiding public transport as a precaution.

Examination:
Neurological examination today is non-focal. Cardiovascular observations are within normal limits. No injuries or post-ictal features.

Investigations:
- Recent bloods (FBC, U&E, LFTs) from GP two weeks ago were within reference ranges per patient report; we have requested the results for confirmation.
- No recent EEG or MRI since prior review; we will consider repeat studies if functional impact continues despite sleep regularisation.

Current medication:
- Lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily.
- Levetiracetam 1000 mg twice daily.
No notable adverse effects except intermittent insomnia around travel. She uses melatonin 2 mg prolonged-release intermittently, patient-initiated, with partial benefit.

Management plan discussed with patient:
- Emphasised regular sleep–wake routine, particularly in the 10–14 days following long-haul flights; she will continue a sleep diary alongside the seizure diary kept by her relative.
- Agreed to trial timed light exposure in the mornings and avoidance of late-evening screens for two hours before bed as patient-led measures.
- We discussed that if perceived daytime lapses continue beyond the next month despite improved sleep, we may consider a small up-titration of levetiracetam or addition of a morning-release melatonin regimen; she prefers to defer medication changes today.
- Safety advice reinforced regarding bathing/showering and use of public transport at less busy times while confidence is low; she understands when to seek emergency help.
- We will review in three months, sooner if events escalate or if any generalised convulsive event occurs.

Summary:
Although no events have been documented since the last appointment, she reports a deterioration in perceived control and daily function following cross-time-zone travel, in the context of circadian disruption. Objective frequency over the past year remains as above. We will prioritise stabilising sleep and closely monitor the impact on daytime function before altering treatment.

Yours sincerely,

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis:
Identify explicit frequencies mentioned:
- "one to two generalised tonic-clonic seizures yearly" -> 1 to 2 per year.
- "three - four absences per day" -> 3 to 4 per day (convert hyphen to 'to').
- "clusters of myoclonic jerks and occasional tonic seizures, these occur roughly once a month" -> 1 cluster per month, but number per cluster not specified.
Rule: Select the highest frequency among multiple seizure types. Daily absences (3 to 4 per day) are more frequent than monthly clusters or yearly GTCs. No evidence of 6+ months seizure freedom (indeed ongoing daily absences reported), so not seizure free.
Therefore, final label: 3 to 4 per day.
seizure_frequency_number: She has three - four absences per day.
reference: There has been little alteration in seizure frequency over the past year; she experiences one to two generalised tonic-clonic seizures yearly with the longest seizure-free period being three weeks. No seizures have been recorded since her last appointment
